title: Why we trust the mechanic who refuses to overcharge us
summary: Reflect on what makes us trust certain tradespeople — a mechanic, a plumber, an electrician — even after a single visit, and what that says about how trust is built.
topic: What makes us trust a particular tradesperson after only one visit
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) about a mechanic, plumber, electrician, or other tradesperson you came to trust quickly and why. Use a relative clause, present perfect for accumulated experience, a modal of speculation (must / might / could), and 'however' or 'although'.
hintStarters: The first time my car broke down in Mexico City, the mechanic I was sent to could easily have charged me three times what the repair cost, and instead he…
If I were asked to define professional trust in a single sentence, I would point to a small moment in that workshop when…
Although the obvious sign of an honest tradesperson is supposed to be the bill, what actually convinces us, I think, is something quieter that happens…

hintVocab.meaningVi: trò chuyện làm rõ vấn đề

hintVocab.phrase: to admit you do not know

hintVocab.meaningVi: đưa ra câu trả lời thẳng thắn

hintVocab.phrase: a recommendation by word of mouth
to walk you through the repair
hintVocab.meaningVi: giải thích từng bước sửa chữa cho bạn

hintVocab.phrase: to show you the broken part
hintVocab.meaningVi: cho bạn xem bộ phận hỏng
từ chối làm phần việc không cần thiết

sampleText:
The first time my car broke down in Mexico City, on a back street in Roma Sur, the mechanic I was sent to could easily have charged me three or four times what the repair actually cost. I had clearly arrived as a foreigner who did not know the price of anything, with a car that had a problem I could not name. Instead, Don Rafael walked me into the workshop, lifted the bonnet, asked his apprentice to bring a small torch, and pointed to the part that was broken. He told me the name of the part, the price of the part, and the time it would take to replace it. Then he said: 'You could ask another mechanic if you want a second opinion. I will not be offended.' I had been ready to be quietly cheated. I had not been ready to be treated as an adult who could verify what I was being told.

What I have come to understand in the years since is that the moment I trusted Don Rafael was not when he produced a fair bill. It was earlier — it was when he offered me the choice to leave. He must have known that almost no foreigner who is already standing in a workshop with a broken car is going to walk out and find another mechanic on a Tuesday afternoon. The offer cost him nothing, but it was honest in a particular way that small dishonest gestures cannot fake. A person who is willing to lose your business in order to prove they are not trying to hold you hostage is signalling something that is very hard to fake the other way around. Although a clever cheat could mimic the words, the timing and the absence of pressure are much harder to perform convincingly.

What I have noticed since, in mechanics and plumbers and electricians in several cities, is that the trustworthy ones tend to share a small set of behaviours that have almost nothing to do with the price. They show you the broken part instead of describing it. They admit when they are not sure. They refuse work that is not necessary and tell you so directly. They do not act offended when you ask a sceptical question. However, none of this would matter if it were performed for show. What makes it real is that the same person behaves this way whether you are a regular customer or a stranger who walked in for the first time that morning. Honesty, I have come to think, is not a single moment of generosity. It is a pattern of small refusals to take advantage when you easily could.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Modals of past speculation: 'could have' and 'must have'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'He could easily have charged me three or four times what the repair actually cost' (khả năng đã có) và 'He must have known' (suy đoán chắc chắn) dùng modal + have + past participle để nói về quá khứ. 'Could have' = có khả năng nhưng có thể không xảy ra; 'must have' = gần như chắc chắn đã xảy ra dựa trên bằng chứng.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'He could easily have charged me three or four times what the repair actually cost' (past possibility, not necessarily realised) and 'He must have known' (confident deduction) both use modal + have + past participle for past speculation. 'Could have' = was capable of, but may not have done it; 'must have' = almost certainly happened based on evidence.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional with 'were' in formal style
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'None of this would matter if it were performed for show' dùng 'were' (chứ không phải 'was') sau 'if' với chủ ngữ số ít — đây là dạng giả định trang trọng (subjunctive) trong điều kiện loại 2. Cả 'was' và 'were' đều được chấp nhận trong tiếng Anh hàng ngày, nhưng 'were' nghe trang trọng và mạnh mẽ hơn trong văn nghị luận.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'None of this would matter if it were performed for show' uses 'were' (rather than 'was') after 'if' with a singular subject — this is the formal subjunctive form in the second conditional. Both 'was' and 'were' are accepted in everyday English, but 'were' sounds more formal and emphatic in argumentative writing.

sampleTranslationVi:
Lần đầu tiên xe tôi hỏng ở Mexico City, trên một con phố nhỏ ở Roma Sur, người thợ sửa xe tôi được giới thiệu đến có thể dễ dàng tính cho tôi gấp ba bốn lần chi phí sửa chữa thật sự. Tôi rõ ràng đã đến như một người nước ngoài không biết giá của bất cứ thứ gì, với một chiếc xe có vấn đề tôi không thể gọi tên. Thay vào đó, Don Rafael dẫn tôi vào xưởng, mở nắp ca-pô, bảo người học việc mang đến một cái đèn pin nhỏ, và chỉ vào bộ phận bị hỏng. Ông nói cho tôi tên của bộ phận, giá của bộ phận, và thời gian cần để thay thế nó. Sau đó ông nói: 'Anh có thể hỏi một thợ khác nếu muốn ý kiến thứ hai. Tôi sẽ không phật lòng.' Tôi đã sẵn sàng để bị lừa một cách lặng lẽ. Tôi chưa sẵn sàng được đối xử như một người trưởng thành có thể xác minh điều mình đang được nói.

Điều tôi đã dần hiểu trong những năm kể từ đó là khoảnh khắc tôi tin Don Rafael không phải khi ông đưa ra hoá đơn công bằng. Đó là sớm hơn — đó là khi ông cho tôi lựa chọn rời đi. Ông ấy hẳn đã biết rằng gần như không có người nước ngoài nào đã đứng trong một xưởng với một chiếc xe hỏng sẽ bước ra ngoài và tìm một thợ khác vào chiều thứ Ba. Đề nghị đó không tốn của ông ấy gì, nhưng nó trung thực theo một cách đặc biệt mà những cử chỉ không trung thực nhỏ không thể giả mạo. Một người sẵn sàng mất khách hàng của bạn để chứng minh họ không cố giữ bạn làm con tin đang phát đi tín hiệu về điều gì đó rất khó giả mạo theo chiều ngược lại. Mặc dù một kẻ lừa đảo khôn ngoan có thể bắt chước lời nói, thời điểm và sự vắng mặt của áp lực thì khó diễn thuyết phục hơn nhiều.

Điều tôi đã nhận thấy kể từ đó, ở các thợ sửa xe, thợ ống nước và thợ điện ở vài thành phố, là những người đáng tin có xu hướng chia sẻ một bộ nhỏ các hành vi mà gần như không liên quan đến giá. Họ cho bạn xem bộ phận hỏng thay vì miêu tả nó. Họ thừa nhận khi họ không chắc. Họ từ chối công việc không cần thiết và nói thẳng với bạn. Họ không tỏ ra phật lòng khi bạn đặt câu hỏi hoài nghi. Tuy nhiên, không điều nào trong số này có ý nghĩa nếu nó được diễn ra để khoe khoang. Điều khiến nó thực sự là cùng một người ứng xử cách này dù bạn là khách quen hay người lạ vừa bước vào lần đầu tiên sáng hôm đó. Sự trung thực, tôi dần nghĩ, không phải là một khoảnh khắc hào phóng đơn lẻ. Đó là một mô hình của những lần từ chối nhỏ không lợi dụng khi bạn có thể dễ dàng làm vậy.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'could have + past participle' for past possibility?
options: He could easily charged me three or four times what the repair actually cost.
He could easily have charge me three or four times what the repair actually cost.
He could easily have charged me three or four times what the repair actually cost.
He could easily had charged me three or four times what the repair actually cost.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Could have + past participle' is the structure for past possibility (he was capable of doing it but did not). 'Have' is the bare infinitive after the modal, followed by the past participle.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal + 'have' + past participle for past possibility.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'must have + past participle' for past deduction?
options: He must has known that almost no foreigner is going to walk out.
He must have knew that almost no foreigner is going to walk out.
He must known that almost no foreigner is going to walk out.
He must have known that almost no foreigner is going to walk out.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Must have + past participle' is the structure for confident past deduction. Past participle of 'know' is 'known'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Must have + past participle'; past participle of 'know' is 'known'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the second conditional with formal 'were' correctly?
options: None of this would matter if it were performed for show.
None of this will matter if it were performed for show.
None of this would matter if it would be performed for show.
None of this would mattered if it were performed for show.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional with formal 'were' = 'if + were, would + base verb'. 'Will' in the main clause and 'would be' in the if-clause are both wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: Formal second conditional uses 'were' for all subjects in the if-clause; main clause uses 'would + base verb'.

options: Although a clever cheat could mimic the words, but the timing is much harder to perform convincingly.
Although a clever cheat could mimic the words, the timing is much harder to perform convincingly.
Although, a clever cheat could mimic the words the timing is much harder to perform convincingly.
A clever cheat could mimic the words, although however the timing is much harder to perform convincingly.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no 'but', no comma after 'although', no doubling with 'however'.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'although' alone; do not pair with 'but' or 'however'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the past perfect correctly?
options: I had ready to be quietly cheated.
I have been ready to be quietly cheated at that point.
I had been ready to be quietly cheated.
I had being ready to be quietly cheated.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect = 'had + past participle'; past participle of 'be' is 'been'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'tend to + base verb' correctly?
options: The trustworthy ones tend share a small set of behaviours.
The trustworthy ones tend sharing a small set of behaviours.
The trustworthy ones tend to sharing a small set of behaviours.
The trustworthy ones tend to share a small set of behaviours.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tend to + base verb' is the standard structure for general patterns; '-ing' and missing 'to' are both wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Tend to + base verb' for general tendencies.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'whether' to express alternatives?
options: The same person behaves this way whether you are a regular customer or a stranger.
The same person behaves this way whether you are a regular customer or a stranger or not.
The same person behaves this way if you are a regular customer or a stranger.
The same person behaves this way whether you are a regular customer or if you are a stranger.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whether A or B' presents two parallel options; 'if' does not work here because it introduces a condition, not equivalent alternatives.
mcQuestions.hint: Use 'whether A or B' for parallel alternatives, not 'if'.

options: They show you the broken part instead to describe it.
They show you the broken part instead of describing it.
They show you the broken part instead of describe it.
They show you the broken part instead describing of it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Instead of + -ing form' is the fixed structure for contrasting an alternative; 'instead to + infinitive' is incorrect.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Instead of' is followed by the -ing form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'take advantage of' correctly?
options: A pattern of small refusals to take the advantage when you easily could.
A pattern of small refusals to take advantage of when you easily could of.
A pattern of small refusals to take advantage when you easily could.
A pattern of small refusals to taking advantage when you easily could.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Take advantage' here is used without an explicit object (the object is implied). 'To + base verb' is correct after 'refusals'; 'taking' is wrong.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Refusal to + base verb'; 'take advantage' is the fixed phrase.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'cost + indirect object + nothing' correctly?
options: The offer cost to him nothing.
The offer cost for him nothing.
The offer was costed him nothing.
The offer cost him nothing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Cost' takes two objects directly: 'cost someone something'. No preposition is needed; 'cost' is irregular (past form same as base).
mcQuestions.hint: 'Cost + indirect object + direct object'; no preposition.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'come to think' correctly?
options: Honesty, I have come to think, is not a single moment of generosity.
Honesty, I have come think, is not a single moment of generosity.
Honesty, I have come to thinking, is not a single moment of generosity.
Honesty, I came to think, is not a single moment of generosity now.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have come to + base verb' is the standard structure for a gradual realisation still relevant now. 'To thinking' is wrong; option D mixes past simple with 'now'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Have come to + base verb' for present, continuing realisations.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If trust is signalled by small refusals to take advantage rather than by promises or marketing, why do so many businesses still try to build trust mainly through what they say rather than what they refuse to do?
